judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently hit all four expectations with nearly identical substance and file/line citations; they are comparable in completeness and rigor, differing mainly in stylistic framing (batches vs. named engineers).

report_1.evidence: Batch B — registry (F-02, F-05, F-09). Owns pipeline/registry.py. All three findings edit this one 11-line file, so they must belong to one engineer

report_2.evidence: Engineer C — pipeline/registry.py (F-02, F-05, F-09). Three one-line changes to one 12-line file: add the aurora-1 endpoint, bump DEFAULT_RUBRIC_VERSION to v3, point DEFAULT_DATASET at fixtures/eval_set.jsonl... Splitting these across people would be three PRs conflicting on adjacent lines for trivial gain.

report_1.evidence: Ordering: all four batches run in a single parallel wave, because after the by-file grouping they are fully file-disjoint... Batch C — rubric parsing (F-01 + F-11)... go to the same engineer, test-first

report_2.evidence: Ordering: all four streams start in parallel on day one, branching from f3cac92 on main, because the file sets are disjoint... Engineer B — pipeline/rubric.py (F-01, F-11).

report_1.evidence: F-06 — escalate, don't assign... Whether and how much to tighten is a product/eval-owner decision, not something an engineer should guess. Flag it as a decision request in parallel; don't block the campaign on the answer.

report_2.evidence: F-06 — a policy decision disguised as a bug... This needs the reviewer or a product owner to specify the new values (ideally justified against data) before anyone codes it

report_1.evidence: An isolated test database: export RESULTS_DB to a private per-engineer path before running tests, because the default in tests/conftest.py:4 is the shared /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db and parallel suite runs against it would collide.

report_2.evidence: A per-engineer RESULTS_DB environment variable pointing at a private path, since tests/conftest.py defaults to the machine-global /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db.
